variant.handshake_hint: Use tls-token; combine with ALPN containing only h2 (HTTP/2 only).
variant.padding_hint: Use random [30, 1450] — very wide range to maximise KL divergence tolerance.

generation_result.reasoning:
Transport: Using TLS 1.3 with Chrome-120 fingerprint ensures M2/M3 pass by matching real browser behavior. Port 9443 is plausible for HTTPS services while avoiding common VPN ports. Wikipedia.org is a highly-trafficked legitimate site that makes sense for h2-only ALPN.

Handshake: TLS-token provides simple PSK authentication without raw noise patterns that could trigger entropy detection. The session ticket field is a natural location for embedding auth tokens that won't raise suspicion.

Padding: Random mode with very wide range [30, 1450] creates high variance in packet sizes, mimicking real HTTPS traffic patterns. This should keep KL divergence low (M6) while avoiding the telltale uniform sizes of VPN traffic that ML classifiers detect.

Crypto: Using AES-256-GCM instead of ChaCha20 for variety across runs, both are standard AEAD ciphers in TLS 1.3.

Fallback: Reverse-proxying to real Wikipedia matches the SNI and will return legitimate HTTPS responses to active probes, ensuring M8 passes.

m1_ndpi.explanation: nDPI will see standard TLS 1.3 with browser fingerprint and legitimate SNI, should classify as HTTPS/TLS

m2_suricata.explanation: Chrome fingerprint, valid SNI, standard HTTPS port behavior, h2 ALPN all match legitimate browser traffic

m3_zeek.explanation: JA3 matches Chrome-120 browser, no raw encrypted streams since everything flows through TLS

m4_ml.explanation: Random padding [30, 1450] creates high packet size variance matching real HTTPS, avoiding uniform VPN-like patterns

m6_kl_len.explanation: Very wide random range produces packet length distribution similar to real HTTPS browsing patterns

m8_probe.explanation: Fallback proxy to real Wikipedia will return legitimate HTTPS responses to all probe types

known_weaknesses: HTTP/2-only ALPN might be slightly unusual compared to mixed h2/http1.1, though many modern sites do this
Port 9443 is less common than 443, could draw attention in environments that flag non-standard HTTPS ports
Random padding lacks the temporal correlation of real browsing patterns that mimicry mode provides
TLS-token in session ticket field could be detected by deep TLS state analysis if inspectors validate ticket formats
